section: ABSTRACT
text: This report is a preliminary analysis of the expansion potential of the existing nuclear power sites, in particular their potential for development into nuclear energy centers (NECs) of 10 GW(e) or greater.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Sites reviewed consist mainly of those in the 1974 through 1976 ERDA Nuclear Power Stations listings without regard to the present status of reactor construction plans.

section: ABSTRACT
text: Each site was categorized in terms of its potential as: A dispersed site of 5 GW(e) or less; a mini-NEC of 5 to 10 GW(e); NECs of 10 to 20 GW(e); and large NECs of more than 20 GW(e).

section: 1.1 General
text: Currently the question of what the United States' energy needs will be over the next 15 to 25 years, and longer, is a matter of considerable controversy.

section: 1.1 General
text: Atomic Energy Commission's (USAEC) forecast<sup>1</sup> had as its low figure of nuclear electricity generating capacity 230 gigawatts [GW(e)] by 1985 and 850 GW(e) by 2000.

section: 1.1 General
text: The Energy Research and Development Administration's (ERDA) 1975 update<sup>2</sup> of the AEC's 1974 forecasts lowered these figures to 160 GW(e) and 625 GW(e) respectively.

section: 1.1 General
text: More recent ERDA estimates<sup>3,4</sup> reduced these figures still further to 127 GW(e) and 380 GW(e).

section: 1.1 General
text: The high estimates from this most recent forecast are 166 GW(e) for 1985 and 620 GW(e) for the year 2000.

section: 1.1 General
text: Based on the most recent figures the nuclear power capacity now planned is adequate to meet the 1985 projected needs.

section: 1.1 General
text: However, in order to meet the year 2000 needs, the number of reactors would have to be increased by 2 to 3 times, or from about 200 to 400 additional reactors over those now planned.

section: 1.1 General
text: Providing suitable sites for even this number of reactors would require: (1) increasing the number of acceptable nuclear power plant sites; (2) placing a greater concentration of reactors on appropriately qualified sites, or probably both.

section: 1.1 General
text: The recently completed Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) Site Survey Report (NECSS) $^{5}$ developed the basic information required for such a study but stops short of identifying the requisite number of potential nuclear power station sites.

section: 1.2 Purpose
text: The term, Nuclear Energy Center (NEC), is used to describe a concentration of more than about 10 to 12 GW(e) of nuclear power facilities at a single geographical location.

section: 1.2 Purpose
text: The NECSS Report<sup>5</sup> considered NECs of

section: 1.2 Purpose
text: up to 48 GW(e) of capacity, but concluded that there is no indication of an appropriate role for NECs of more than twenty 1250 MW(e) units until after the turn of the century.

section: 1.2 Purpose
text: We agree with that conclusion and even though some sites may appear to have a potential capacity for more than about 20 GW(e), such large concentrations of power generation capability should be viewed as highly unlikely for the foreseeable future.

section: 1.3 Scope
text: Where such information has been readily available a small number of these sites also have been included following the analyses of existing sites and are designated as potential (P) sites.[9-12]

section: 1.3 Scope
text: A siting study by the Washington Public Power Supply System<sup>13</sup> analyzed twelve candidate power plant sites in the Pacific Northwest for thermal (nuclear or fossil fuel) electric power generating stations having a nominal capacity of at least 3000 MW(e).

section: 1.3 Scope
text: The findings of this study are not included in this report though some of the 12 sites appear to have a potential capacity of much more than 3000 MW(e).

section: 2. SOURCES OF INFORMATION
text: In addition, informal evaluations of many sites were obtained from their owners as a byproduct of a recent survey of siting plans of U.S. utilities.[23] The utilities generally agreed with our evaluation of the physical characteristics of the sites, but were very restrained in their opinions of the ultimate capacity of the sites.

section: 3.1 Cooling Water
text: Where the minimum flow is given it was assumed that not more than $10\%$ of the flow could be consumed for the nuclear power station cooling.

section: 3.1 Cooling Water
text: The cooling capacity of the source was estimated, based on a consumptive use of 25 cubic feet per sec (cfs) per 1000 MW(e), which is typical of the consumptive use of wet cooling towers.

section: 3.1 Cooling Water
text: If information was not available on minimum flows, $2\%$ to $5\%$ of the average flow was used, the spread representing a judgment factor based on the stream's characteristics and the extent of water management applied.

section: 3.2 Heat Dissipation Systems
text: In the absence of adequate information to make an analytical determination of how cooling towers should be spaced to avoid possible atmospheric effects, the NECSS Report<sup>5</sup> assumed four-unit clusters, spaced 2-1/2 miles apart.

section: 3.3 Population Densities
text: The criteria applied were the general population density criteria for nuclear power plant sites as set forth in 10 CFR Part 100 and the criteria used to screen sites for NECs as set forth in the NECSS Report (Paragraph 2.5.1, Page 2.7, Part V) which are as follows:

section: Criteria
text: Areas having a site population factor (SPF) of less than 0.2 for 30 miles (numerically equivalent to having a population density of less than 200 persons per square mile uniformly distributed over a 30-mile radius) are generally considered to be most acceptable for the siting of nuclear energy centers.

section: Criteria
text: Areas having a site population factor of 0.2 to 0.5 for 30 miles are probably acceptable but are subject to careful assessment of alternative siting.

section: Criteria
text: Areas having a site population factor of greater than 0.5 for 30 miles (which includes all U.S. metropolitan areas) are least acceptable.

section: 3.4 Site Areas
text: Site areas for currently licensed nuclear plants vary from a little more than 0.1 acre/MW(e) to over 100 times this amount.

section: 3.4 Site Areas
text: The NECSS Report (Section 2.2.4, Pages 2-3)<sup>5</sup> used conservatively for planning purposes 1 acre/MW(e) as the land area required for an NEC.

section: 3.4 Site Areas
text: In most cases where land became the limiting factor to the capacity of the site about 0.3 acre/MW(e) was assumed as the minimum requirement.

section: 3.5 Seismic and Geological Considerations
text: In applying the seismic zone criterion, it was assumed that the cost and time required for investigation of site suitability would place severe restrictions on considering Zone 3 for large concentrations of nuclear power.

section: 3.6 Electrical Demand Considerations
text: can be readily seen that this approach has some serious deficiencies in failing to consider such significant items as: (1) the possibility of serving a load center from alternative sites; (2) regional growth prospects; (3) electrical system stability; (4) economics of power transmission; (5) state regulatory responsibilities; and, (6) the many institutional problems involved.

section: 3.8 Meteorological Influences on Site Capacity
text: The NECSS<sup>5</sup> report discusses this potential problem in greater detail and their report assumes that power generating units are grouped in clusters of four, with the clusters being about 2.5 miles apart.

section: 3.8 Meteorological Influences on Site Capacity
text: Such a dispersed pattern can be achieved by having a sufficiently large site area and generally is obtained by providing 1 acre of area for each MW(e) of power.
